question: Workers on a large Zeebe cluster see noticeable delays — on the order of seconds — before a new job is picked up, even with long polling enabled. Which change does the docs recommend?

options.text: Enable job streaming so jobs are pushed from the broker to workers instead of relying on per-partition long polling.
Increase the long-polling timeout so each poll waits longer for jobs to appear.
Have each worker poll every partition more aggressively (shorter interval) so new jobs are found faster.
Reduce the number of partitions in the cluster so there are fewer endpoints for the worker to poll.

a.text: The docs' performance best-practice recommendation for reducing job activation latency is to enable job streaming. Long polling forces workers to periodically poll every partition; on large clusters this adds delay in the order of seconds, which job streaming eliminates.

b.text: Longer poll timeouts only change how long an idle request waits; they do not solve the per-partition polling overhead the docs call out.

c.text: More aggressive polling is precisely what the docs warn against — it increases broker load when no jobs are available without actually solving the latency on large clusters.

d.text: Reducing partition count is a major topology change with throughput and fault-tolerance trade-offs; the docs do not propose it as the latency fix here.

explanation: On large clusters, long polling forces workers to poll every partition and balance aggression with broker load, which can add delays measured in seconds. The docs' explicit performance best-practice fix is to enable job streaming, so jobs are pushed to workers as soon as they appear.
